Name: task_relevant_mutual_info_compression
Title: Compression for Generalization: Optimizing Task-Relevant Mutual Information
Short Hypothesis: Explicitly optimizing for task-relevant mutual information during model compression leads to better generalization performance compared to minimizing distortion alone, as it ensures the compressed model retains only the information necessary for the task.
Related Work: Recent work in information-theoretic model compression focuses on rate-distortion bounds (e.g., 'Lossy Compression with Gaussian Diffusion') and generalization bounds (e.g., 'Rate-Distortion Theoretic Generalization Bounds for Stochastic Learning Algorithms'). However, these approaches do not explicitly optimize for task-relevant mutual information. Our proposal bridges this gap by aligning compression with the information bottleneck principle, ensuring that the compressed model retains only the information necessary for the task.
Abstract: Model compression is a key technique for deploying deep learning models in resource-constrained environments. However, existing compression methods often focus on minimizing distortion or maximizing compression efficiency, without explicitly considering the task-relevance of the retained information. We propose a novel approach that optimizes for task-relevant mutual information during compression, ensuring that the compressed model retains only the information necessary for the task. We derive theoretical bounds on the bits-per-weight needed to preserve predictive sufficiency and validate our approach empirically on standard benchmarks. Our results show that optimizing for task-relevant mutual information leads to better generalization performance compared to minimizing distortion alone, providing a tighter operational bound on compression efficiency.
Experiments: Compress ResNet-18 on CIFAR-10 using our proposed method and compare generalization performance with traditional quantization and pruning baselines.
Evaluate the trade-off between compression ratio and task-relevant mutual information on ImageNet using a pre-trained ViT model.
Measure the impact of task-relevant mutual information optimization on robustness to distribution shifts using the WILDS benchmarks.
Compare the proposed method with state-of-the-art compression techniques in terms of bits-per-weight and generalization error.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Estimating task-relevant mutual information accurately may be challenging, especially for high-dimensional models.
The proposed method may introduce additional computational overhead during compression, though this is mitigated by using efficient estimators.
The theoretical bounds may be difficult to derive for complex architectures, requiring simplifying assumptions.
The approach may not generalize well to tasks where the task-relevant information is not easily separable from noise.
